blocks:
Sūrat al-Fātiḥah

Meccan, and it is said Medinan, and it is said that it was revealed twice: once in Mecca and once in Medina. It consists of seven verses.

بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful)

(1) In al-Tawhid and Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): Allah is He to Whom every creature turns in worship (يَتَأَلَّهُ إِلَيْهِ) during needs and hardships, when hope is cut off from every direction other than Him, and the means are severed from all besides Him. You say: بِسْمِ اللهِ (In the Name of Allah), meaning: I seek help in all my affairs through Allah, the One for Whom worship is not rightful except for Him, the Helper when help is sought, and the Responder when He is called upon. I say: The meaning of يَتَأَلَّهُ إِلَيْهِ is: He is the One to Whom one flees, seeks refuge, and finds tranquility. And in another narration from him (peace be upon him): It means, "By this Name, read and perform this action." In al-Uyun and al-Ma'ani, narrated from al-Rida (peace be upon him): It means, "By this, I mark my soul with one of the marks (السمات) of Allah, which is worship." He was asked: "What is the mark (السمة)?" He replied: "The sign (العلامة)." In al-Tawhid and Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from him (peace be upon him): A man said to al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "O son of the Messenger of Allah, guide me to Allah, what is He? For the disputants have multiplied against me and confused me." He said: "O servant of Allah, have you ever ridden a ship?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Did it ever break down with you where there was no ship to save you and no swimming to suffice you?" He said: "Yes." He said: "Did your heart cling there to the idea that something among things was capable of rescuing you from your predicament?" He said: "Yes." Al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "That thing is Allah, the One capable of rescue when there is no rescuer, and of aid when there is no helper." Another narration concerning the meaning of Allah will come in the interpretation of Sūrat al-Ikhlāṣ, if Allah wills. And narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): Allah is the greatest Name among the Names of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic; it is not fitting that anyone other than Him be named by it. And narrated from him (peace be upon him): الرَّحْمنِ (the All-beneficent) is He Who shows mercy by expanding provision upon us. And in a narration: He is the One Who is compassionate toward His creation through provision; He does not cut off the sources of their provision even if they cut themselves off from His obedience. الرَّحِيمِ (the All-merciful) toward us in our religions, our worldly life, and our Hereafter. He lightened the religion for us and made it easy and light (خفيفاً). He shows mercy to us by distinguishing us from His enemies. I say: The provision (رزق) of every creature is that by which the stability of its existence and its appropriate perfection are maintained. Thus, the Raḥmāniyyah mercy (الرحمة الرحمانية) encompasses all existing things and includes all blessings, as Allah, the Glorified, said: أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ ثُمَّ هَدى (He perfected everything that He created, then He guided it). As for the Raḥīmiyyah mercy (الرحمة الرحيمية), meaning success (التوفيق) in this world and the religion, it is specific to the believers. What has been narrated regarding its inclusion of the disbelievers is only from the perspective of inviting them to faith and religion, such as what is found in Tafsir al-Imam (peace be upon him) from their saying (peace be upon them): al-Raḥīm (the All-merciful) toward His believing servants by lightening His acts of obedience for them, and toward His disbelieving servants by being gentle in inviting them to agree with Him. Hence, al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: الرَّحْمنِ (the All-beneficent) is a specific name for a general attribute, and الرَّحِيمِ (the All-merciful) is a general name for a specific attribute. And Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him) said: al-Raḥmān is the Raḥmān of the world, and al-Raḥīm is the Raḥīm of the Hereafter—meaning concerning the affairs of the Hereafter. Both narrations are reported in al-Majma', and in al-Kafi, al-Tawhid, al-Ma'ani, and al-Ayyashi, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): The Bā' (الباء) is the Splendor (بهاء) of Allah, the Sīn (السين) is the Loftiness (سناء) of Allah, and the Mīm (الميم) is the Glory (مجد) of Allah. And in a narration: The Dominion (ملك) of Allah, and Allah is the God (إله) of everything. al-Raḥmān (the All-beneficent) toward all His creation, and al-Raḥīm (the All-merciful) toward the believers specifically. And al-Qummi narrated from him (peace be upon him): The like of it, but only with the last narration. And it is narrated in al-Mishkāt, cited in al-Majma', from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: That Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, possesses one hundred mercies. He sent down one of them to the earth and divided it among His creation, and by it they show affection and mercy to one another. And He reserved ninety-nine for Himself, by which He will show mercy to His servants on the Day of Resurrection. And it is narrated: That Allah will gather this one to those to complete them to one hundred, by which He will show mercy to His servants on the Day of Resurrection. In Tafsir al-Imam is the meaning found in the two narrations from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): And the Tasmiyah (i.e., بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ) at the beginning of every sūrah is a verse from it. The end of a sūrah was only known by its revelation beginning the next one. And Allah did not reveal any Book from the heaven except that this was its opening (فاتحته). This is narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) by al-Ayyashi. In al-Kafi, narrated from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): The beginning of every Book revealed from the heaven is بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful). So, if you recite it, do not worry about not seeking refuge (تستعيذ), for when you recite it, it will shield you between the heaven and the earth. In al-Uyun, narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): That it (the Tasmiyah) is part of al-Fātiḥah, and that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله used to recite it and count it as a verse from it, and he would say: Fātiḥat al-Kitāb is al-Sab' al-Mathānī (السبع المثاني - The Seven Oft-Repeated Verses). And in it (al-Uyun) and in al-Ayyashi, narrated from al-Rida (peace be upon him): That it is closer to the Greatest Name of Allah (اسم الله الأعظم) than the pupil of the eye is to its white part. And it is narrated in al-Tahdhib from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him)... And al-Qummi narrated from him: That it is the most deserving thing to be recited aloud (يجهر به), and it is the verse about which Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: وَإِذا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ وَحْدَهُ وَلَّوْا عَلى أَدْبارِهِمْ نُفُوراً (And when you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, they turn their backs in aversion). In al-Khiṣāl, narrated from him (peace be upon him): That reciting it aloud (الإجهار بها) in the prayer is obligatory (واجب). And al-Ayyashi narrated from him (peace be upon him), who said: "What is wrong with them? May Allah fight them! They deliberately targeted the greatest verse in the Book of Allah and claimed that it is an innovation (بدعة) if they recite it openly." I say: He means the general populace (العامّة). Narrated from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): They stole a verse from the Book of Allah: بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful). And it is proper to recite it when commencing every matter, great or small, so that blessing (بركة) may be placed in it. In al-Kafi, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "Do not abandon it, even if what follows it is poetry." In al-Tawhid and Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from him (peace be upon him): Whoever among our followers (شيعتنا) abandons it, Allah will test him with something disliked, so as to alert him to gratitude (الشكر) and praise (الثناء), and to wipe away the stain (وصمة) of his shortcoming due to his abandonment of it. And narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): That the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله informed me from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, that He said: "Every matter of consequence (ذي بال) in which بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ is not mentioned is cut off (أبتر)."

(1) الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ (All praise belongs to Allah) Meaning: [Praise belongs to Allah] for what Allah has bestowed upon us in blessings. In al-Uyun and Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): He was asked about its interpretation, and he said: "It is that Allah made His servants know some of His blessings upon them generally, since they are unable to know all of them in detail, because they are too numerous to be counted or known. So He said: 'Say الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ (All praise belongs to Allah) for what He has bestowed upon us in blessings.'" In al-Kafi, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): Allah does not bestow a blessing upon a servant, whether small or great, and the servant says الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ (All praise belongs to Allah), except that he has rendered its gratitude (شكرها). رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ (Lord of the worlds): In al-Uyun and Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): Meaning: The Possessor (مالك) of the groups (الجماعات) of every creature, and their Creator, and the Driver of their provisions to them from where they know and from where they do not know. He turns the living creatures (الحيوانات) in His power, nourishes them from His provision, surrounds them with His protection (كنفه), and manages each one according to its benefit. He holds the inanimate objects (الجمادات) by His power, and He holds what is connected among us from collapsing, and what is collapsing from sticking together, and the heaven from falling upon the earth except by His permission, and the earth from sinking except by His command.

footnote (١) It is said that the reason for writing the *Basmalah* (*البسملة*) with the omission of the *alif* (*الألف*), contrary to the established rule of script, is the frequency of its usage, and the lengthening of the *Bā'* (*الباء*) compensates for it. [End of quote] from him (may Allah sanctify his secret).

footnote It is narrated: That Quraysh used to write *بِسْمِكَ اللَّهُمَّ* (In Your Name, O Allah) during the *Jāhiliyyah* (Age of Ignorance), until Sūrat Hūd was revealed, containing: **بِسْمِ اللهِ مَجْراها وَمُرْساها** (In the Name of Allah be its sailing and its anchoring). Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم was commanded to write **بِسْمِ اللهِ** (In the Name of Allah). Then, after that, it was revealed to him: **قُلِ ادْعُوا اللهَ أَوِ ادْعُوا الرَّحْمنَ أَيًّا ما تَدْعُوا فَلَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى** (Say, 'Invoke Allah or invoke the All-beneficent. Whichever you invoke, to Him belong the Best Names'). Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم was commanded to write **بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ** (In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent). Then, when Sūrat al-Naml was revealed: **إِنَّهُ مِنْ سُلَيْمانَ وَإِنَّهُ بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ** (It is from Solomon, and it begins with 'In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful'), the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم was commanded to write that at the beginning of books and the start of letters. And it is a verse from every *sūrah*. And our saying: **بِسْمِ اللهِ** (In the Name of Allah) means: I begin with the Name of Allah, or my beginning is with the Name of Allah, so it is the predicate (*خبر*) of an omitted subject (*مبتدأ محذوف*). The derivation of the word *al-Ism* (*الاسم*) is from *al-Sumuww* (*السمو*), which is loftiness (*العلو*) and elevation (*الرفعة*). From this is derived *samā al-zar'u* (*سما الزرع*), meaning the crop grew high and rose. And from this is the derivation of *al-Samā'* (*السماء* - the heaven) due to its height and elevation. And it is said that it is derived from *al-Simah* (*السمة*), which is the sign (*العلامة*), so it is as if it is a sign for that for which it was designated. [End of quote] from him (may Allah sanctify his secret).

(٢) الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (2) the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. It is said that perhaps the repetition of the two (names) is to draw attention, through them, within the context of the mentioned attributes, to His deservingness of praise (الحمد). (٣) مالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ (3) the Master of the Day of Retribution. In Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from the Imam (peace be upon him): It means the One Who is Capable of establishing it (the Day) and the Judge therein with truth (الحق), retribution (الدين), and reckoning (الحساب). And it was recited as ملك يوم الدين (King of the Day of Retribution). Al-Ayyashi narrated that al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) recited it countless times. And in Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, He said: "The most intelligent of the intelligent is he who takes account of himself and works for what comes after death, and the most foolish of the foolish is he who lets his soul follow its desires (هواه) and wishes vain hopes (الأماني) upon Allah, the Exalted." And in another narration (حديث آخر): "Take account of yourselves before you are taken account of, and weigh them (your deeds) before they are weighed." I say: And in both of them (the narrations) is an indication that it is incumbent upon every human being to complete his reckoning (حسابه) and the weighing of his deeds (وزن عمله) in the abode of this world (دار الدنيا) such that he does not need them in the Hereafter (الآخرة). And this is indeed the case according to those possessing intellect (اولي الألباب).

(4) إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ (You [alone] do we worship)

(1) In Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from the Imam (peace be upon him): Allah the Almighty said: Say, O creation upon whom favors have been bestowed: ** إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ (You [alone] do we worship), O Bestower of favors upon us, we obey You sincerely (مخلصين), affirming Your Oneness (موحّدين), with humility (التذلل) and submission (الخضوع), without hypocrisy (رياء) or seeking reputation (سمعة).

And in a general (عاميّة) narration from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): It means: We desire none other than You from You. We do not worship You for recompense (العوض) or exchange (البدل), as those ignorant of You (الجاهلون بك) and veiled from You (المغيبون عنك) worship You.

I say: The servant only transitioned from the third person (الغيبة) to direct address (الخطاب) because, through his glorification of Allah, the Exalted, he was drawing near to Him, gradually progressing until he reached a station of proximity where knowledge became for him direct observation (عياناً), report became witnessing (شهوداً), and absence (الغيبة) became presence (حضوراً).

وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ (2) (and You [alone] do we ask for help): [We seek help] in Your obedience and Your worship, and in repelling the evils of Your enemies, warding off their plots (مكائدهم), and remaining steadfast upon what You have commanded. Thus is it stated in Tafsir al-Imam (peace be upon him).

It is said: The pronoun hidden (المستتر) in ** نَعْبُدُ and ** نَسْتَعِينُ refers primarily to the reciter, those with him among the guardian angels (الحفظة), and those present for the congregational prayer (صلاة الجماعة). And for the rest of the affirmers of Oneness (الموحدين), [the reciter] includes his worship within the folds (تضاعيف) of their worship and mixes his need with their need, hoping that it may be accepted through their blessing (ببركتها) and answered along with theirs. For this reason, the congregation (الجماعة) was legislated. And ** إِيَّاكَ was placed first for the purpose of glorifying Him (التعظيم له), emphasizing Him (الاهتمام به), and indicating exclusivity (الحصر).

(5) **اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ ** (5) Guide us to the straight path.

In al-Ma'ani and Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): It means: "Guide us to adhere to the path that leads to Your love, conveys us to Your Paradise, and prevents us from following our desires, lest we perish (فَنَعْطَب), or adopting our own opinions, lest we be destroyed (فَنَهْلِك)."

And narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): It means: "Perpetuate for us Your divine aid (تَوْفِيق) by which we obeyed You in the past days of our lives, so that we may likewise obey You in the future of our lives."

I say: Since the servant is in need of guidance in all his affairs moment by moment (آناً فآناً) and instant by instant (لحظة فلحظة), the perpetuation (إِدَامَة) of guidance is another guidance following the initial guidance. Thus, interpreting guidance as its perpetuation is not a departure from the apparent meaning of the word (ظَاهِر اللَّفْظ).

And narrated from him (peace be upon him): The Straight Path (الصراط المستقيم) in this world is that which falls short of extremism (الغلوّ), rises above negligence (التقصير), and is upright (استقام). And in the Hereafter, it is the path of the believers to Paradise.

In al-Ma'ani, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): It is the path to the knowledge (معرفة) of Allah. They are two paths (صراطان): a path in this world and a path in the Hereafter. As for the path in this world, it is the Imam whose obedience is obligatory (المفترض الطاعة). Whoever recognizes him in this world and follows his guidance will pass over the Path (الصراط) which is the bridge (جسر) of Hell in the Hereafter. And whoever does not recognize him in this world, his foot will slip from the Path in the Hereafter, and he will fall (فَتَرَدَّى) into the Fire of Hell.

And narrated from him (peace be upon him): Verily, the Path (الصراط) is the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him).

And in another narration: And the knowledge (معرفته) of him.

And in another: That it is the knowledge (معرفة) of the Imam. And in another: We are the Straight Path (الصراط المستقيم).

And al-Qummi narrated from him (peace be upon him): The Path (الصراط) is finer than a hair and sharper than a sword. Among them are those who pass over it like lightning, and among them are those who pass over it like the running of a horse, and among them are those who pass over it walking, and among them are those who pass over it crawling (حَبْواً), and among them are those who pass over it clinging (مُتَعَلِّقاً), so the Fire takes something from him and leaves something.

And in another narration: That it is dark, and people strive upon it according to the measure of their lights (أَنْوَارِهِم).

I say: And the ultimate outcome (مَآل) of all these interpretations is one, according to those who possess knowledge (العارفين) of their secrets (أَسْرَارِهِم).

And its explanation, according to your level of understanding (فَهْمِك), is that every human being, from the beginning of his existence (ابْتِدَاء حُدُوثِهِ) until the end of his life, undergoes innate internal transitions (انتقالات جبلية باطنية) in perfection (الكمال), and natural and psychological movements (حركات طبيعية ونفسانية) that arise from the repetition of deeds (تكرار الأعمال), and from which spiritual stations (المقامات) and states (الأحوال) arise. Thus, he continuously moves from one form (صورة) to another, from one disposition (خُلُق) to another, from one belief (عقيدة) to another, from one state (حال) to another, from one station (مقام) to another, and from one perfection (كمال) to another, until he connects with the Intellectual World (العالم العقلي) and the Near Ones (المقربين), and joins the Exalted Assembly (الملإ الأعلى) and the Foremost Ones (السابقين), if divine aid (التوفيق) assists him and he is among the perfect ones (الكاملين); or he joins the People of the Right Hand (أصحاب اليمين) if he is among the moderate ones (المتوسطين); or he is resurrected with the devils (الشياطين) and the People of the Left Hand (أصحاب الشمال) if the Devil takes him as a guardian (وَلَّاه) and abandonment (الخذلان) accompanies him in the ultimate outcome (المآل). This is the meaning of the Straight Path (الصراط المستقيم). And part of it is that which, if traversed, leads him to Paradise, and this is what the Divine Law (الشرع) encompasses, as Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has said: وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ (And indeed you guide to a straight path,) the Path of Allah, which is the path of Divine Unity (التوحيد), knowledge (المعرفة), moderation (التوسط) between opposites (الأضداد) in character (الأخلاق), and adherence to righteous deeds (صوالح الأعمال).

In summary: The form of guidance (صورة الهدى) that the believer establishes for himself while he is in the abode of this world, following the guidance (هُدَى) of his Imam therein, is finer than a hair and sharper than a sword in its meaning (في المعنى). It is dark; no one is guided to it except he for whom Allah has appointed a light by which he walks among people. People strive upon it according to the measure of their lights (أَنْوَارِهِم).

It is narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): That the human form (الصورة الانسانية) is the Straight Path (الطريق المستقيم) to all good, and the bridge (الجسر) extended between Paradise and the Fire.

I say: Thus, the Path (الصراط) and the one traversing it (المار عليه) are one thing. In every step, he places his foot upon his head—meaning, he acts according to the requirement of the light of his knowledge (نور معرفته), which is in the position of his head. Rather, he places his head upon his foot—meaning, he builds his knowledge (معرفته) upon the result of his deed, the foundation of which was based on previous knowledge, until he traverses the stations (المنازل) to Allah. And to Allah is the ultimate return (المصير).

It has become clear from this that the Imam is the Straight Path (الصراط المستقيم), and that he walks uprightly (سَوِيّاً) upon the Straight Path, and that the knowledge (معرفة) of him is the knowledge of the Straight Path and the knowledge of walking upon the Straight Path. And verily, whoever recognizes the Imam and walks upon his path, swiftly or slowly, according to the measure of his light and his knowledge (معرفته) of him, will succeed by entering Paradise and being saved from the Fire. And whoever does not recognize the Imam does not know what he is doing, so his foot slips and he falls (تَرَدَّى) into the Fire.

(٦) صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ : (١) (The path of those whom You have blessed)

In al-Ma'ani and Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): Meaning, say: "Guide us to the path of those whom You have blessed with success (التوفيق) in Your religion and Your obedience, not with wealth and health, for they may be disbelievers (كفّاراً) or transgressors (فساقاً)."

And He (peace be upon him) said: They are those concerning whom Allah the Almighty said: ** وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَداءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ وَحَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً.** (69) Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle—they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous, and excellent companions are they!

(٧) غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ (Not of those who are subject to [Your] wrath): He said: They are the Jews, concerning whom Allah said: مَنْ لَعَنَهُ اللهُ وَغَضِبَ عَلَيْهِ (those whom Allah has cursed and with whom He is wrathful). وَلَا الضَّالِّينَ (nor of those who are astray): He said: They are the Christians, concerning whom Allah said: قَدْ ضَلُّوا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَأَضَلُّوا كَثِيراً (They have certainly gone astray before, and led many astray). And it is added in Tafsir al-Imam (peace be upon him): Then the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said: Everyone who disbelieves in Allah is subject to wrath (مَغْضُوبٌ عَلَيْهِ) and astray (ضَالٌّ) from the path of Allah. In al-Ma'ani, narrated from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Household and grant them peace): الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ (those whom You have favoured) are the followers (شِيعَة) of Ali (peace be upon him), meaning: You have favoured them with the guardianship (وِلَايَة) of Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him); You were not wrathful toward them, nor did they go astray. And from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): It means Muhammad and his progeny (ذُرِّيَّتُهُ). And al-Qummi narrated from him (peace be upon him): That الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ (those who are subject to wrath) are the Nussab (النُّصَّاب), and الضَّالِّينَ (those who are astray) are the people of doubts (أَهْلُ الشُّكُوك) who do not recognize the Imam (الامام). I say: And into the path of those favoured enters every moderation (وَسَط) and uprightness (اسْتِقَامَة) in belief or action, for they are الَّذِينَ قالُوا : رَبُّنَا اللهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقامُوا (those who say, 'Our Lord is Allah,' and then remain steadfast). And into the path of those subject to wrath enters every negligence (تَفْرِيط) and shortcoming (تَقْصِير), especially if it is done knowingly, as the Jews did with Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad. And into the path of those astray enters every excess (إِفْرَاط) and exaggeration (غُلُوّ), especially if it is done out of ignorance, as the Christians did with Jesus. This is because wrath necessitates distance (البُعْد) and expulsion (الطَّرْد), and the one who falls short (المُقَصِّر) is the one who turns away and avoids, so he is the distant one. And straying (الضَّلَال) is the absence from the intended goal (المَقْصُود), and the one who exceeds (المُفْرِط) is the one who approaches and oversteps, so he is the one from whom the desired goal (المَطْلُوب) is absent. And al-Ayyashi narrated from the Prophet (may Allah bless him and his Household and grant them peace): That the Mother of the Book (أُمّ الكِتَاب) is the best Surah that Allah revealed in His Book, and it is a cure for every ailment except al-Samm (السَّام), meaning death. In al-Kafi, narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him): Whoever is not cured by al-Hamd (الحَمْد), nothing will cure him. And from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): If al-Hamd (الحَمْد) were recited over a dead person seventy times, and the soul were then returned to him, it would not be strange. And in a narration: That it is among the treasures of the Throne (العَرْش). In al-Uyun and Tafsir al-Imam, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), from his forefathers, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon them), he said: I heard the Messenger of Allah (may Allah bless him and his Household and grant them peace) saying: Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: I have divided the Opening of the Book (فَاتِحَة الكِتَاب) between Me and My servant. Half of it is for Me, and half of it is for My servant, and My servant shall have what he asks for. When the servant says: بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful), Allah, Glorious is His Majesty, says: My servant has begun with My Name, and it is incumbent upon Me to complete his affairs for him and to bless his circumstances for him. When he says: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ (All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds), Glorious is His Majesty, says: My servant has praised Me and knows that the blessings he possesses are from Me, and that the afflictions that have been averted from him are through My grace. I call you to witness that I shall add to his blessings of this world the blessings of the Hereafter, and I shall avert from him the afflictions of the Hereafter just as I averted from him the afflictions of this world. And when he says: الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (the All-beneficent, the All-merciful), Allah, Glorious is His Majesty, says: My servant has testified for Me that I am the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. I call you to witness that I shall surely make his share of My bounty abundant and his portion of My giving generous. When he says: مالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ (the Master of the Day of Retribution), Allah, the Exalted, says: I call you to witness that just as he has acknowledged that I am the Master of the Day of Retribution, I shall surely make his reckoning easy on the Day of Account, and I shall accept his good deeds and overlook his evil deeds. When the servant says: إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ (You alone do we worship), Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: My servant has spoken truly; he worships Me alone. I call you to witness that I shall reward him for his worship with a reward that everyone who opposed him in his worship of Me will envy. When he says: وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ ** (and to You alone do we turn for aid), Allah, the Exalted, says: He sought aid through Me and took refuge in Me. I call you to witness that I shall surely aid him in his affair, and I shall relieve him in his hardships, and I shall take his hand on the day of his calamities. When he says: اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ (Guide us on the straight path) until the end of the Surah, Allah, Glorious is His Majesty, says: This is for My servant, and My servant shall have what he asked for. I have answered My servant and given him what he hoped for, and granted him security from that which he feared (وَجَل).

footnote (١) And if you recognize the Path (*الصِّرَاط*), then cross it in this world, and take this [lesson] from one who passes over the Path clinging [to it], whom the Fire has seized, [but] Allah saved him from the Fire and gathered him with the righteous (*الأَبْرَار*) and the virtuous (*الأَخْيَار*). From him (may his secret be sanctified).
